posts.body:
Ok so the Twilight council banish action reads:

"Battle. Your hits force enemy warriors to move instead, ignoring rule. Ignore any rolled his you take"

So if I have partisans or any other means to deal extra hits, do those hits also make enemies move, or to they hit them normally? 

Also can you use that action to hit enemy cardboard? If so, do you hit them normally or do extra rules apply?

Thanks in advance

1) Extra Hits you deal will also make enemy warriors move

2) Banish does not deal hits to enemy buildings and tokens. If you move all enemy warriors and have hits left over, those left over hits are simply unused

OmegasSquared wrote:

1) Extra Hits you deal will also make enemy warriors move

2) Banish does not deal hits to enemy buildings and tokens. If you move all enemy warriors and have hits left over, those left over hits are simply unused

Ok ty

Also if you deal multiple hits, can you force and opponent to move to two different clearings?

DGD_Kami wrote:

OmegasSquared wrote:

1) Extra Hits you deal will also make enemy warriors move

2) Banish does not deal hits to enemy buildings and tokens. If you move all enemy warriors and have hits left over, those left over hits are simply unused

Ok ty

Also if you deal multiple hits, can you force and opponent to move to two different clearings?

No, Banish moves all hit warriors to a single destination

2) Banish does not deal hits to enemy buildings and tokens. If you move all enemy warriors and have hits left over, those left over hits are simply unused

Also if you deal multiple hits, can you force and opponent to move to two different clearings?

No, Banish moves all hit warriors to a single destination

Excelent tysm

Also if you deal multiple hits, can you force and opponent to move to two different clearings?

No, Banish moves all hit warriors to a single destination

Even though its a much simpler system than it once was, it's still a little complex and very unique. Banish is so weird that I wish all of this was on the player board instead of just in the law. This is an instance where more would be more. A lot of people I've play Root with only check the Law for edge cases and trust the boards to (succinctly) cover what they need to know. Or they play most factions first in the app and then have no true understanding of how the minutiae of factions work. For some factions, the board is enough, mostly because the faction is very simple or comes from earlier days of Root when you could just take most instructions at face value. For others, the board is inadequate - someone needs to know the Law or know when to use the Law. After all, you don't know what you don't know. I find this to be especially true for Lord of Hundreds and all three Homeland factions. These are also factions with the most dense text, though, and I wish that it had been broken up a bit more. I can picture the Woodland Alliance board in my head. The same for the Keepers in Iron. There's a shape to their turn structure that helps you get through it. The Knaves have that a bit, but it's not quite as visually supported as the Vagabond's board. Even just having the buildings on the board, such as with the Lizard Cult, is a huge help for giving the rest of the turn a form you can live in comfortably. It would seem that I'm arguing for less text, but I am really arguing for graphic design that allows for more text because the user can situate different text in diverse contexts. Every time I see white space on a player board filled with a big animal picture opposite a wall of instructions, I just know I'm going to have to put extra effort into walking someone through their turn (or working to internalize that turn if I'm playing as that faction). Again, not a dig on the factions. Just a phenomenon that I wish was better addressed given how complex these factions are.

DMoneyMonet wrote:

For others, the board is inadequate - someone needs to know the Law or know when to use the Law. After all, you don't know what you don't know. I find this to be especially true for Lord of Hundreds and all three Homeland factions. 

What is lacking from the LoH board? What rules do you think should be there but aren't?

stuffbybez wrote:

DMoneyMonet wrote:

For others, the board is inadequate - someone needs to know the Law or know when to use the Law. After all, you don't know what you don't know. I find this to be especially true for Lord of Hundreds and all three Homeland factions. 

What is lacking from the LoH board? What rules do you think should be there but aren't?

Precisely the fact that you cannot hit cardboard with banish.

I know the Law of root has it, but come on this should be at least in the manual

I know the Law of root has it, but come on this should be at least in the manual 

For what it's worth, the Banish text OP posted is not the final board text. The final text for Banish says "Battle. Ignored rolled hits you take. You only hit warriors, forcing them to move as you want, ignoring rule."
